danger_signals.meaning: Heavy escalation of danger; stage environmental hazards

danger_signals.meaning: Critical threat requiring pattern recognition and special tactics

Knife and projectile animations from enemy sprites

Safe zones and healing opportunities within levels

safety_signals.meaning: Gun and knife invulnerability; temporary damage immunity

safety_signals.visual_form: Final stage asset appearing after general defeat

world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Relentless enemy waves; military-trained soldier patterns; escalating vehicle deployment

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Stage 1 POW camp cells; confined room layouts
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player begins in prison; must break free and fight through four escalating facility types

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Stage progression: camp → warehouse → jungle → command base; increasing enemy density and vehicle types
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Enemy soldiers change types (shotgun, frogmen, strongman); vehicle threats increase in later stages

world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Final escape sequence triggered; mission victory achieved

environmental_storytelling.example: Four-stage linear progression (POW camp → warehouse → jungle → enemy base) maps political geography: captivity site → supply lines → hostile terrain → command authority. Each environment visually communicates escalation.
Frogmen appear in jungle stage (NES); shotgun wielders and armored soldiers in base—visual environmental fit indicates military unit specialization and deployment logic.
Assault choppers, armored trucks, motorcycles appear during stages—vehicular threats communicate supply routes, mobile defense, and military infrastructure strength in each location.
POW camp confined cells → open warehouse → natural jungle → fortified command base. Architecture progression mirrors player agency: from imprisoned to free movement to reclaiming enemy territory.
NES version huts/rooms containing health, brass knuckles, and armor signal civilian or supply infrastructure within military zones—safe pockets within hostile world.

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Continuous waves; attempt to block player forward progress; engaged until defeated
Ranged threat pattern; requires evasion or close combat tactics
Specialized aquatic/jungle unit; visual indicator of environmental threat adaptation
High durability; requires pattern learning and combo attacks
Ultimate threat; defeat triggers extraction and mission success
Airborne threat; requires grenade use (special mechanic introduced for this encounter)
Heavy vehicle threat; grenade-dependent strategy; final vehicular obstacle before general
Progressively stronger as weapon pickups (knife, machine gun) or powerups (brass knuckles, armor) are collected

notes: P.O.W. is a Cold War beat 'em up where visual manifestation of world-state relies heavily on location-based storytelling and enemy type diversity. The game communicates geopolitical tension through architecture (captivity → escape → assault). NES version introduces environmental powerups in rooms, adding safe-zone mechanic not in arcade. Character colors (blue player 1, red player 2 arcade) provide faction clarity in multiplayer. Vehicular escalation and boss fights mark state transitions. The extraction chopper serves as primary victory signal.
